docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This page defines the primary audiences of the handbook and clarifies how different readers should approach the material. A systems engineering handbook becomes more useful when it is explicit about who it serves, what prior knowledge it assumes, and what kind of value each audience should expect to obtain from it.

docs.title: Early-Career Systems Engineers
docs.text: Readers entering the discipline often encounter fragmented definitions of systems engineering. For this audience, the handbook provides structure, terminology, and lifecycle context. It is particularly useful for understanding how stakeholder needs become requirements, how requirements influence architecture, and how architecture must ultimately be supported by integration and V&V evidence.

docs.title: Practicing Systems Engineers
docs.text: For engineers already working in the discipline, the handbook serves as a reference framework. It can support the design of project documentation, technical review logic, artifact sets, and governance mechanisms. It is especially relevant for engineers seeking a more consistent way to connect method, deliverables, and lifecycle decision points.

docs.title: Technical Leads and Chief Engineers
docs.text: Technical leaders often need a concise but rigorous way to communicate expectations to teams. This handbook can support discussions on baselines, technical authority, decision governance, configuration control, review readiness, and evidence quality.

docs.title: Multidisciplinary Team Members
docs.text: Software engineers, hardware engineers, test engineers, safety engineers, product owners, and operations personnel may all interact with systems engineering outputs without being systems engineers themselves. For these readers, the handbook helps explain the role and rationale of artifacts such as ConOps, requirements baselines, interface definitions, risk registers, and verification matrices.

docs.title: Students and Researchers
docs.text: The site can also be used as a structured learning scaffold. While it is not a formal academic textbook, it can support coursework, project-based learning, and self-study by showing how conceptual topics map to project artifacts and lifecycle activities.

docs.title: Secondary Audiences
docs.text:
The handbook may also be useful for:

  • project managers seeking a clearer view of engineering maturity and review readiness
  • quality and assurance personnel who need visibility into technical baselines and evidence structures
  • documentation architects and knowledge managers building an engineering portal
  • organizations establishing or refining an internal systems engineering playbook

docs.title: Assumed Background
docs.text: The handbook assumes a general familiarity with engineering work and technical projects, but it does not assume deep prior knowledge of systems engineering. Some sections are introductory, while others are written with a more advanced tone appropriate for readers who already understand the lifecycle and artifact logic of complex systems.

docs.text:
Start with:

  1. discipline/what-is-systems-engineering.md
  2. discipline/systems-thinking.md
  3. lifecycle/lifecycle-overview.md
  4. requirements/requirements-engineering-overview.md
  5. architecture/architecture-principles.md
  6. case-study/overview.md

docs.title: If You Need to Build Documentation
docs.text:
Start with:

  1. preface/conventions-and-notation.md
  2. appendix/document-style-guide.md
  3. artifacts/
  4. reference/glossary.md

docs.title: Limits of Audience Coverage
docs.text: No single handbook can fully satisfy every possible role. Some readers will require more depth in regulatory compliance, formal modeling, safety certification, or domain-specific design practices than this project currently provides. The present objective is to support a broad but coherent systems engineering audience while leaving room for later specialization.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This handbook exists to provide a coherent, professional, and reusable reference framework for systems engineering. Its role is not limited to explaining terminology. It is intended to articulate how systems engineering operates as a unifying discipline across the lifecycle of a complex system, from mission need to retirement.

The handbook addresses a recurring problem in engineering organizations: technical knowledge is often fragmented across presentations, local conventions, disconnected templates, and individual experience. As a result, teams may understand their own specialty well while lacking a shared view of how requirements, architecture, risk, interfaces, verification, validation, and operations fit together.

This site is designed to reduce that fragmentation.

docs.title: Why This Handbook Is Needed
docs.text:
Complex systems rarely fail because a single component is poorly engineered in isolation. More often, failure emerges from weak coordination between needs, assumptions, interfaces, constraints, decisions, evidence, and operational realities. Systems engineering exists to manage precisely that problem.

A handbook is useful when an organization or a project needs to answer questions such as:

  • What is the difference between a stakeholder need and a system requirement?
  • When should an architecture be considered baselined?
  • How should verification evidence be linked to requirements?
  • What artifacts are needed before a major design review?
  • How are technical decisions recorded and governed?
  • How should the engineering team reason about change, risk, and configuration?

This handbook is structured so that these questions are answered systematically rather than informally.

docs.title: Primary Objectives
docs.text: The handbook has five principal objectives.

docs.title: Establish a Common Engineering Language
docs.text: Complex projects require a vocabulary that is both shared and precise. Terms such as function, component, interface, constraint, validation, and baseline are frequently used inconsistently. The handbook seeks to define such terms in context and to apply them consistently across sections.

docs.title: Connect Concepts to Process
docs.text: It is not enough to define concepts in isolation. Readers should understand when a method is applied, what inputs it requires, what outputs it creates, who owns the work, and how the result influences downstream lifecycle activities.

docs.title: Connect Process to Artifacts
docs.text: Engineering work becomes durable when it is reflected in artifacts that can be reviewed, baselined, changed, and traced. The handbook therefore treats documents and records as part of the engineering method rather than as administrative by-products.

docs.title: Connect Artifacts to Governance
docs.text: Complex systems require controlled decision-making. This means that technical content must be reviewed, approved, versioned, and linked to explicit authority. The handbook clarifies how technical work participates in governance through reviews, baselines, change control, and evidence management.

docs.title: Connect Theory to a Realistic System Context
docs.text: A central case study—the Autonomous Infrastructure Inspection Drone System—anchors the handbook. This system is rich enough to illustrate operational context, stakeholder diversity, performance requirements, interface complexity, risk, and verification logic without becoming so large that the example loses clarity.

docs.title: Scope of the Handbook
docs.text:
The handbook covers the following broad areas:

  • systems engineering fundamentals
  • lifecycle-oriented development
  • requirements engineering
  • architecture and system decomposition
  • modeling and MBSE concepts
  • trade studies and engineering analysis
  • integration planning and interface control
  • verification and validation strategy
  • risk and opportunity management
  • configuration and change control
  • quality, reviews, and engineering governance
  • operations, sustainment, and obsolescence
  • artifacts, templates, and case-study application

docs.title: What the Handbook Is Not
docs.text:
This handbook is not intended to be:

  • a replacement for project-specific procedures
  • a substitute for formal standards, regulations, or contractual obligations
  • a vendor-specific tool manual
  • a deep specialist guide in every adjacent domain, such as software engineering, avionics certification, or detailed safety regulation

Instead, it acts as a systems-level reference layer that helps align and integrate those domains.

docs.title: Intended Outcome
docs.text:
A successful use of this handbook should produce three results.

First, the reader should gain a clearer understanding of the intellectual role of systems engineering.

Second, a project team should be able to derive from the site a more structured and defensible set of engineering practices, artifacts, and review expectations.

Third, the case study should demonstrate how a disciplined engineering framework can be instantiated in a concrete system context.

docs.title: Relationship to the Repository
docs.text: Because the handbook is implemented in MkDocs and maintained in GitHub, it is also an example of documentation as an engineering product. Its structure, naming conventions, cross-links, and revision history are part of its value. The repository is therefore not merely a storage location for prose; it is an environment in which the engineering logic of the handbook can evolve in a controlled way.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
This handbook covers the systems engineering discipline at a professional and lifecycle-oriented level. It addresses how complex systems are defined, decomposed, analyzed, governed, integrated, verified, validated, operated, and sustained. The emphasis is on coherence across engineering activities, not on deep specialization in any single adjacent discipline.

The scope includes:

  • conceptual foundations of systems engineering
  • requirements engineering and traceability
  • system architecture and decomposition
  • model-based perspectives at an introductory level
  • engineering analysis and trade studies
  • integration planning and interface control
  • verification, validation, and evidence logic
  • risk and opportunity management
  • configuration, baselines, and change control
  • quality, reviews, and governance
  • operations, support, and obsolescence
  • artifacts and documentation structures
  • application to the AIIDS case study

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
The handbook has important limits.

It does not provide domain-complete guidance for certification, airworthiness approval, regulatory compliance, contractual flow-down, or tool-specific implementation. It also does not attempt to replace formal standards or organizational procedures.

In addition, the case study is intentionally scoped to remain understandable and reusable. It therefore simplifies certain real-world concerns such as procurement constraints, manufacturing qualification detail, regulatory approval pathways, and detailed safety assurance cases.

docs.title: Intended Use Within These Limits
docs.text:
The content should be used as:

  • a reference framework
  • a structured teaching and learning baseline
  • a documentation architecture for a project repository
  • a professional example of systems-engineering-oriented knowledge organization

It should not be used uncritically as a complete substitute for project tailoring, formal standards interpretation, or contractual engineering obligations.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This page introduces the role of requirements engineering within the systems engineering lifecycle. It explains why requirements matter, how they relate to stakeholder needs and architecture, and what makes a requirements baseline useful rather than merely verbose.

docs.title: Why Requirements Matter
docs.text:
Requirements are one of the primary instruments by which engineering intent becomes explicit, reviewable, and testable. Without disciplined requirements engineering, systems tend to accumulate ambiguity, inconsistent expectations, hidden assumptions, and weak evidence logic.

A requirement is not simply a statement of preference. It is a controlled expression of capability, performance, constraint, or condition that the system must satisfy.

docs.title: Requirements in Context
docs.text:
Requirements engineering sits between stakeholder understanding and system realization.

It begins with the recognition that stakeholders express needs in operational language. These needs may be incomplete, aspirational, context-dependent, or inconsistent. Requirements engineering translates those needs into forms that can be analyzed, allocated, baselined, traced, and verified.

This means requirements engineering has relationships with:

  • stakeholder analysis
  • ConOps development
  • operational scenarios
  • architecture and function allocation
  • interface definition
  • risk management
  • verification and validation planning
  • configuration and change control

docs.title: Classes of Requirements
docs.text:
The handbook will later distinguish among multiple requirement classes, including:

  • functional requirements
  • performance requirements
  • interface requirements
  • environmental requirements
  • operational requirements
  • safety-related requirements
  • constraints and assumptions expressed in requirement-adjacent form

This classification matters because different requirement types influence different downstream artifacts and verification methods.

docs.title: Qualities of Good Requirements
docs.text:
A useful requirement should be:

  • clear in meaning
  • bounded in scope
  • free of unnecessary design prescription unless design is intentional
  • testable or otherwise verifiable
  • traceable to an originating need, decision, or constraint
  • consistent with related requirements
  • stable enough to baseline, while still open to controlled change

Poor requirements create downstream uncertainty. Good requirements do not remove all ambiguity from a project, but they make ambiguity visible and governable.

docs.title: Requirements and Traceability
docs.text:
Traceability is essential because requirements are not isolated sentences. A requirement should be traceable backward to a need, scenario, assumption, decision, or regulatory driver, and forward to architecture, interfaces, verification methods, and evidence.

A requirement without traceability is difficult to justify. A design without traceability is difficult to defend. Evidence without traceability is difficult to trust.

docs.title: Requirements and the Case Study
docs.text: In the AIIDS case study, requirements will connect the inspection mission to concrete system expectations. Examples will eventually include endurance, communication range, payload performance, geolocation accuracy, safety behavior, environmental operability, and operator interaction constraints. These requirements will then be allocated across airborne, ground, and communication elements.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: Introduce Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA) as a structured method to identify potential failures and their impact.

docs.title: FMEA Structure
docs.text: Item Failure Mode Effect Cause Severity Occurrence Detection RPN

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Failure Mode: how something can fail
  • Effect: consequence of the failure
  • Cause: root cause
  • Severity (S): impact of failure
  • Occurrence (O): likelihood
  • Detection (D): ability to detect before failure
  • RPN = S × O × D

docs.title: Example
docs.text: Item Failure Mode Effect Cause S O D RPN Battery Power loss Mission abort Degradation 9 3 4 108

docs.title: Engineering Value
docs.text:
• early failure identification
  • prioritization of mitigation
  • design improvement guidance

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This page defines the verification test levels used in the project. Test levels organize verification activities according to the maturity of the integrated system and the scope of behavior under examination.

docs.title: Context
docs.text: For the Autonomous Infrastructure Inspection Drone System, verification cannot rely on a single type of test. The system combines airborne functions, ground interaction, communications, payload data acquisition, safety logic, and operator supervision. As a result, testing must be staged across multiple levels.

docs.title: Test Level Model
docs.text:
The project adopts four practical verification levels:

  1. Element / Component Level
  2. Subsystem Level
  3. Integration Level
  4. System / Mission Level

docs.title: Level 1 — Element / Component Level
docs.text:
This level verifies the behavior of individual components or tightly bounded elements.

Typical examples include:

  • battery endurance characterization
  • communication module basic throughput checks
  • sensor operational confirmation
  • payload image capture functionality
  • ground interface display element behavior

docs.title: Objectives
docs.text:
• confirm basic functionality
  • identify local defects early
  • reduce risk before subsystem integration

docs.title: Level 2 — Subsystem Level
docs.text:
This level verifies grouped elements that together perform a recognizable subsystem responsibility.

Typical subsystem examples include:

  • Flight Control System
  • Navigation Subsystem
  • Payload System
  • Communication System
  • Ground Control Station

docs.title: Objectives
docs.text:
• confirm subsystem behavior
  • verify local interfaces
  • establish readiness for broader integration

docs.title: Level 3 — Integration Level
docs.text:
This level verifies interactions across subsystem boundaries.

Typical integration focuses include:

  • flight control ↔ navigation consistency
  • payload ↔ communications interaction
  • airborne ↔ ground station command and telemetry behavior
  • safety logic ↔ flight control contingency activation

docs.title: Objectives
docs.text:
• validate interface behavior
  • identify timing, format, and dependency mismatches
  • confirm coordinated operation under controlled scenarios

docs.title: Level 4 — System / Mission Level
docs.text:
This level verifies the complete operational system in end-to-end conditions that represent mission intent.

Typical system-level examples include:

  • autonomous inspection mission execution
  • post-mission data availability
  • loss-of-link response during mission
  • operator supervision and intervention during autonomous flight

docs.title: Objectives
docs.text:
• demonstrate mission capability
  • confirm operational coherence
  • support validation readiness

docs.title: Test Level Summary
docs.text: Test Level Primary Focus Typical Output Element / Component Local functionality Component test records Subsystem Bounded capability and local interfaces Subsystem verification results Integration Cross-subsystem interaction Interface and integration test evidence System / Mission End-to-end mission behavior System verification reports

docs.title: Allocation Logic
docs.text:
Not every requirement is verified at only one level. Some requirements need layered verification. For example:

  • a communication requirement may be checked at component, integration, and mission levels
  • an autonomy requirement may require subsystem control tests and full mission tests
  • a safety requirement may need analysis, local test, and system-level confirmation

docs.title: Entry and Exit Logic
docs.text: Each test level should have minimum readiness criteria.

docs.title: Entry Criteria Examples
docs.text:
• applicable requirements identified
  • configuration known
  • interfaces defined
  • test procedure approved
  • necessary instrumentation available

docs.title: Exit Criteria Examples
docs.text:
• pass/fail results recorded
  • anomalies captured
  • evidence stored
  • readiness assessed for next level

docs.title: Common Failure Modes
docs.text:
Typical weaknesses include:

  • skipping subsystem verification and going directly to mission tests
  • treating interface failures as isolated defects instead of integration issues
  • assuming component success guarantees system success
  • performing mission tests without stable configuration knowledge

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This page defines the verification philosophy adopted for the project. It establishes the principles used to plan, organize, execute, and review test activities for the Autonomous Infrastructure Inspection Drone System.

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Verification is not treated as a late-stage activity performed after design completion. Instead, it is integrated throughout the system lifecycle and used to confirm that the system baseline remains consistent with approved requirements, architectural choices, interface definitions, and safety expectations.

docs.title: Verification Intent
docs.text:
The verification approach is requirement-driven. Each baselined requirement is expected to be:

  • traceable to a source
  • allocated to one or more system elements
  • associated with one or more verification methods
  • supported by objective evidence

The test philosophy therefore aims to ensure that testing is not an isolated operational exercise, but part of the formal engineering control structure.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
The project applies the following principles:

  1. Verification shall begin during requirements and architecture definition.
  2. Test planning shall be traceable to the requirements baseline.
  3. Test scope shall include nominal and abnormal conditions.
  4. Safety-relevant functions shall receive explicit verification attention.
  5. End-to-end mission behavior shall be tested in addition to subsystem behavior.
  6. Verification evidence shall be reviewable and attributable to a controlled baseline.

docs.title: Verification Methods
docs.text:
The project uses four standard verification methods:

  • Inspection for documentation, configuration, hardware presence, and interface definition checks
  • Analysis for calculations, simulations, timing budgets, and compliance assessments
  • Test for controlled execution and performance measurement
  • Demonstration for operationally realistic confirmation of capability

Test is the primary method for dynamic behavior, but it is not assumed to be sufficient for every requirement.

docs.title: Functional Requirements
docs.text:
Functional requirements are generally verified through:

  • subsystem tests
  • integration tests
  • system-level operational tests
  • demonstrations where operator interaction is involved

docs.title: Performance Requirements
docs.text:
Performance requirements are generally verified through:

  • instrumented tests
  • environmental or endurance evaluations
  • quantitative analysis
  • repeatable measurement conditions

docs.title: Interface Requirements
docs.text:
Interface requirements are generally verified through:

  • ICD inspection
  • compatibility checks
  • interface tests
  • end-to-end communication demonstrations

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
Constraints are generally verified through:

  • compliance inspection
  • documented analysis
  • configuration review
  • supporting evidence packages

docs.title: Evidence Philosophy
docs.text:
Verification evidence must be sufficient to support review, acceptance, and potential audit. Evidence may include:

  • approved test procedures
  • recorded results
  • screenshots or logs
  • measured values
  • checklists
  • compliance notes
  • anomaly reports
  • verification summary statements

docs.title: Treatment of Anomalies
docs.text:
A failed test or unexpected result is not treated as an informal setback. It is treated as engineering evidence that must be analyzed. The project therefore expects:

  • anomaly identification
  • anomaly recording
  • impact assessment
  • retest or re-analysis when required

docs.title: Configuration Control During Testing
docs.text:
Test validity depends on configuration control. All significant verification activities should identify:

  • tested configuration
  • software or firmware status if relevant
  • interface baseline status
  • applicable requirement baseline
  • environmental assumptions

docs.title: Review Criteria
docs.text:
The test philosophy is correctly applied when:

  • every baselined requirement has a credible verification path
  • pass/fail logic is objective
  • subsystem and end-to-end coverage are both present
  • abnormal conditions are not ignored
  • produced evidence is suitable for technical review

docs.title: Common Failure Modes
docs.text:
Typical weaknesses include:

  • testing without requirement linkage
  • overreliance on demonstrations where formal testing is needed
  • missing acceptance thresholds
  • failure to identify test configuration
  • declaring success without evidence retention
